summary: In 1637, William B. Story arrived on the east coast of the New World from Norwich, England. He was a servant to Samuel Dix, a joiner, which suggests he was an apprentice in that trade. William settled in Ipswich, Massachusetts, where he married Sarah Foster in 1640 and together they had nine children. His family eventually spread throughout the northeast of what is now America. Though details are scarce, I believe he was likely a Puritan, contributing to the early colonial community.

Norwich, England, is the place William B. Story and Sarah Foster originally came from before immigrating to the New World. William arrived from Norwich in 1637, and Sarah, also from Norwich, married William in Ipswich in 1640. It seems Norwich was the starting point for their journey to establish a new life in America. This connection to Norwich ties their English roots to their early colonial experience.

The ship called the Rose is one of the vessels William B. Story might have arrived on when he came to the New World in 1637. There is some uncertainty whether it was the Rose or another ship named John and Dorothy, but the Rose is definitely part of the story. This ship represents the journey from England to America during the early colonial period. It symbolizes the start of William’s new life in Ipswich, Massachusetts.

John and Dorothy is another ship that William B. Story might have arrived on in 1637, though there is some uncertainty about this. It is mentioned alongside the Rose as possible vessels that brought him from Norwich, England, to the New World. This ship is part of the early migration story that led William to settle in Ipswich, Massachusetts. It highlights the perilous journey many early settlers made to start new lives.

Samuel Dix was the man William B. Story served as a servant when he first arrived in the New World. Dix was a joiner by trade, so I assume William was apprenticed to him to learn the joinery craft. This relationship likely helped William establish himself in Ipswich, Massachusetts, and contribute to the building of the new community. Samuel Dix represents the early colonial trades and mentorship that supported settlement growth.

Ipswich, Massachusetts, is where William B. Story settled after arriving in 1637. It was also where he married Sarah Foster in 1640, and together they raised nine children. Ipswich was part of the early New England colonies, likely settled under Puritan influence, including by John Winthrop the Younger. This town was the foundation of William’s family and their spread throughout the northeast.

Sarah Foster was an English settler from Norwich who married William B. Story in Ipswich, Massachusetts, in 1640 when she was 20 years old. Together, they had nine children and started a family that spread throughout the northeast of what is now America. Sarah represents the early colonial women who helped establish families and communities in New England. Her marriage to William ties their English origins to the colonial experience.

New England is the region where William B. Story and his family eventually settled and spread throughout after arriving in the 1630s. Ipswich, Massachusetts, was one of the early towns in this area, founded under Puritan influence. The region was shaped by settlers like William and Sarah who came from England seeking religious freedom and new opportunities. New England’s history is deeply connected to these early colonial families and their trades.

Puritanism was likely the religious belief that brought William B. Story to the New World, as he settled in Ipswich, Massachusetts, a town founded by Puritan settlers. Although I’m not certain, it makes sense given the time period and place, since Ipswich was settled by John Winthrop the Younger, son of the famous Puritan leader John Winthrop. Puritanism shaped the early colonial communities and their values, influencing how people like William lived and worked. It was a major factor in the colonization of New England in the 1600s.

John Winthrop the Younger was the founder of Ipswich, Massachusetts, where William B. Story settled after arriving in 1637. He was the son of John Winthrop, a key Puritan leader, and played a role in establishing Puritan communities in New England. This connection suggests that William’s settlement in Ipswich was influenced by the Winthrop family’s leadership and Puritan ideals. John Winthrop the Younger’s role helps explain the religious and social context of William’s life.

John Winthrop was the father of John Winthrop the Younger and a major Puritan leader who helped shape the early New England colonies. His influence extended to towns like Ipswich, Massachusetts, where William B. Story settled. The Puritan values and leadership of John Winthrop likely set the religious and social framework that guided settlers like William. Understanding John Winthrop’s role helps me see the bigger picture of why people came to the New World in the 1630s.
